body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

body.title: Updates to the CDDL grammar of RFC 8610
Application-Oriented Literals in CBOR Extended Diagnostic Notation
A feature freezer for the Concise Data Definition Language (CDDL)
Concise Binary Object Representation (CBOR) Tags for Time, Duration, and Period
A Concise Binary Object Representation (CBOR) of DNS Messages
CDDL 2.0 and beyond -- a draft plan

processor that compares multiple redefinitions of the same thing and make sure they are all the same that mechanism is triggered and you you can make sure that the parts of your specification are consistent and that's why it's good to have something like include import will never use a second definition of of the same thing so that's the main difference and that's why you use include within your own project where you want to make sure that all the parts that you define in your project actually are consistent and you use import when when you pull something from a library where you really only want to pull the the part that you are interested in thank you so one one other nice aspect of import is that it's not possible to make a recursion Loop uh which include doesn't give you Okay so we've got uh we'll be posting a couple of messages to the list about the adoptions to confirm that that's good uh Chris lemons did you uh want to say anything about your comment yet go ahead yeah sure the the uh one of the things that we're going to keep in mind when we're looking at modules is the ability uh to import and or include from documents that are not necessarily rfcs uh cbdl is useful for uh defining structures in a variety of places and so we want to make sure that that um that is at least as useful as uh what we see with like the import from you know rsc9090 or whatever the number is yeah the the current definition cheats a little bit on this so the thing to the right of an import is a name and how that name is mapped into something is defined by the tool so cdlc just takes an environment variable that looks like a Unix path command line path and checks whether the file is is in one of these positions and one of the positions and the last one is a collection of data that the tool has imported uh from from the rfcs that have been published so if you are working with a draft you would make sure that the recent copy of that draft lies around in your file system at a place that you reference in this Unix path and you would get the right draft but it's it's really too hard we found out to to specify what version exactly you want to have because if if a draft is moving forward and is referencing other draft and so on the the only real way you can get a specification that doesn't break everyone every time anyone moves is to put those files into a directory on your file system that is constructed in a particular way and I don't expect that that we will be able to do this in the language but I would love would love to learn about good ways to do this uh yeah and when I I'll continue to uh redress with these thoughts in mind and uh uh we can discuss it as the work progresses but this is all going in good directions so thank you yes so uh one thing that I actually do is I housing the whole set of internet drafts there are only about 2 000 active drafts so that's not not a big collection um and that is one of the directories that is in the the introopers okay uh we have one more thing we need to deal with before we wrap up um getting the bi-weekly meeting schedule going um I've had a chat with Marco to coordinate us with core and we are on the same schedule as we have been which means that the next meeting will be on the 19th of April and then we'll go every two weeks after that until ietf 117 does anybody have a problem with that that
